title: Ricci flows with Rough Initial Data
speaker: Peter Topping
tags: Workshop on Geometric Functionals: Analysis and Applications

proof_stages.label: Loss of initial data under metric convergence
proof_stages.summary: The talk first shows that approximating smooth initial metrics only in distance can produce a limiting Ricci flow with the wrong initial condition, and explains that uniform lower Ricci bounds are the needed mechanism for distance control in limits.

proof_stages.label: Rough initial data as noncollapsed Ricci limit spaces
proof_stages.summary: Rough Ricci-flow initial data are formulated as metric spaces whose distance is recovered as t↓0 from a smooth flow, and the relevant class is defined to be noncollapsed pointed Gromov-Hausdorff limits of smooth manifolds with uniform lower Ricci bounds.

proof_stages.label: Regularity conjecture and three-dimensional theorem
proof_stages.summary: The Cheeger-Colding-Anderson-Tian regularity question is specialized to dimension three, where the theorem states that every noncollapsed three-dimensional Ricci limit space is globally homeomorphic, indeed locally bi-Hölder homeomorphic, to a smooth manifold.

proof_stages.label: Ricci-flow regularization strategy and global-flow obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The intended proof is to flow the Ricci limit space and compare its t↓0 distance with the limiting metric, but ordinary global Ricci flow cannot be expected even for some smooth lower-Ricci manifolds because of possible singularities forming at infinity.

proof_stages.label: Pyramid Ricci flow existence theorem
proof_stages.summary: The obstruction is resolved by constructing a Ricci flow only on an expanding pyramid-shaped subset of spacetime, with distances converging locally uniformly to a metric isometric to the Ricci limit space and with local bi-Hölder comparison to positive-time metrics.

proof_stages.label: Local Ricci flow with estimates rather than uniqueness
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses local Ricci flows on finite balls, adopting the philosophy that one sacrifices uniqueness and instead constructs solutions satisfying curvature and Ricci lower-bound estimates sufficient for compactness.

proof_stages.label: Pyramid extension lemma
proof_stages.summary: A key extension lemma supplies compatible local Ricci flows on nested balls with constants and lifetimes depending only on coarse lower-Ricci and noncollapsing data, allowing finite pyramid Ricci flows to be built recursively.

proof_stages.label: Compactness limit and recovery of the initial metric space
proof_stages.summary: Applying the finite-pyramid construction to the smooth approximating manifolds and using the uniform pyramid shape gives compactness and a limiting pyramid Ricci flow, while lower Ricci bounds and the double-bootstrap technique ensure that the limit actually attains the original Ricci limit space as its initial data.

examples.name: Flat torus sequence losing its initial condition
examples.note: A sequence of smooth Ricci flows on T^2 has initial distance metrics converging uniformly to the flat unit-area torus but converges for positive time to the static flow 2g_0, showing why metric convergence alone is insufficient and motivating lower Ricci bounds.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the torus sequence through metric convergence, curvature estimates, subsequential Ricci-flow convergence, and identifies the limiting flow as static 2g0 rather than g0

passes.reason: A pathological but non-sporadic sequence of torus Ricci flows is used to refute that metric convergence of initial data, even with C/t curvature control, forces the limit flow to attain that initial condition.
It uses a flat homogeneous torus limit to show that mere metric convergence of initial data need not be preserved by limiting Ricci flows.
Centered on the homogeneous flat torus, the sequence is used to refute naive preservation of metric initial data under limiting Ricci flows.
A constructed sequence of otherwise ordinary torus Ricci flows is used to show that metric convergence plus C/t curvature control need not preserve the expected initial condition.

The flat torus setup is used to show metric convergence of smooth initial data and c/t curvature control need not preserve the expected initial condition; the flat target is a highly regular locally symmetric object.
the flat torus limit is highly regular but the sequence is used to show metric convergence of initial data need not give the expected initial Ricci flow.

It is used to show that metric convergence of initial data plus curvature control can still lose the expected initial Ricci-flow limit, while the sequence itself is not specified as structurally special.

It refutes the expectation that metric convergence of initial tori plus c/t curvature control preserves the initial condition, using a degenerating sequence with curvature unbounded below around a symmetric flat torus.
It refutes the expectation that metric convergence of smooth initial data forces the limiting Ricci flow to have that initial condition, using a highly regular flat torus model.
Used to show metric convergence of initial data with good curvature decay can still lose the intended initial condition; the model involves a homogeneous flat torus.
Counterexample to metric convergence preserving Ricci-flow initial data, based on a homogeneous flat torus.

Used to show metric convergence of smooth initial data plus C/t curvature control need not preserve the expected initial Ricci flow; the underlying flat torus is regular but the approximating sequence is not intrinsically sporadic or extremal.

examples.name: Two-dimensional cone with angle less than 2π
examples.note: The cone is smoothed to give a simple noncollapsed Ricci limit space, making pointed Gromov-Hausdorff approximation and topological regularity concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the cone to check Ricci-limit approximability by smoothing, the angle restriction for avoiding negative curvature blow-up, and its topological regularity at the tip

passes.reason: The angle-<2π cone is a rotationally symmetric singular Ricci-limit model offered as a basic positive example, not as an obstruction.
It is a rotationally self-similar cone used as a basic Ricci-limit-space model rather than to refute a claim.
A rotationally symmetric metric cone has a collapsed singular apex, but here it only illustrates a Ricci limit space that is topologically regular.
The angle-<2π cone is a rotationally self-similar model Ricci-limit space illustrating a topologically regular singularity, not refuting the main claim.
The angle-<2π Euclidean cone is a rotationally and scale-symmetric model Ricci-limit singularity, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
a round two-dimensional cone of angle below 2π is a rotationally symmetric model Ricci limit space used illustratively, not adversarially.
It is a standard illustrative Ricci-limit cone, with a collapsed conical tip and rotational symmetry, not a refutation of the main conjecture.
It is used as a basic Ricci-limit-space illustration; structurally it is a rotationally symmetric cone with the link collapsed to a singular apex.
It is used as a basic illustrative Ricci limit space whose cone point is still topologically regular, and the round metric cone is rotationally symmetric.
Introduced as a basic illustrative Ricci limit space rather than to refute a claim; the circular cone is rotationally symmetric.
Rotationally symmetric model Ricci-limit space used illustratively rather than adversarially.

Used as a basic positive example of a noncollapsed Ricci limit space that can be smoothed with lower curvature bound; a round two-dimensional cone is rotationally symmetric but not sporadic or boundary-sharp here.

examples.name: Metric cone over RP^3 and its Euclidean products
examples.note: The cone R^4/(x∼-x) is a four-dimensional Ricci limit space not homeomorphic to a manifold at the vertex, and crossing it with R^{n-4} motivates the expected codimension-four singular behavior in higher dimensions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the RP3 cone as an antipodal quotient, explains how to approximate it using the Eguchi-Hanson metric, checks it is a noncollapsed Ricci limit space, and reasons why it is not a manifold, then extends this by products with Euclidean space

passes.reason: The round RP^3 cone and its Euclidean products are highly symmetric and realize the codimension-four singular threshold while disproving the naive claim that all Ricci limit spaces are manifolds.
The symmetric quotient cone R^4/{±1} and its products realize the critical codimension-four singularity and refute the naive claim that all Ricci limit spaces are manifolds.
The symmetric orbifold cone R^4/±1 and its products have conical codimension-four singularities, giving a sharp obstruction to all Ricci limit spaces being manifolds.
C(RP^3)≅R^4/±1 and its Euclidean products are highly symmetric codimension-four singular models used to disprove that all Ricci limit spaces are manifolds and to show the n−4 threshold is sharp.

The cone C(RP^3) and its Euclidean products are symmetric quotient-cone models realizing codimension-four singular behavior, and are invoked to disprove that all Ricci limit spaces are topological manifolds.
the flat cone R^4/{±1} and its Euclidean products are symmetric codimension-four singular models used to refute the idea that all Ricci limit spaces are manifolds.
The cone R^4/{±1} and its Euclidean products refute the naive manifold-regularity claim and give a symmetric cone singularity realizing the codimension-four threshold.
It disproves the hope that all Ricci limit spaces are manifolds, and its Euclidean products realize the sharp codimension-four singular stratum with a highly symmetric cone quotient.
It is used to disprove the naive claim that all Ricci limit spaces are manifolds, and its Euclidean products give the sharp codimension-four cone-singularity model.
Used to show Ricci limit spaces need not be topological manifolds, with Euclidean products giving the codimension-four threshold singular model.
Counterexample to all Ricci limit spaces being manifolds, realized by a symmetric sharp codimension-four cone model.

Used to refute the idea that every Ricci limit space is homeomorphic to a manifold; the RP^3 cone times Euclidean space is a highly symmetric codimension-four model realizing the sharp singular-stratum threshold.

examples.name: Shrinking S^2-end that pinches instantly at infinity
examples.note: A smooth three-manifold with S^2 cross-sections shrinking along an end has lower Ricci control but cannot plausibly admit a global positive-time Ricci flow, forcing the move to pyramid domains that stop before far-out pinches occur.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the shrinking S2-end under Ricci flow, comparing it to shrinking cylinders and showing why arbitrarily far-out necks would pinch before any positive existence time

passes.reason: The S^2-horn has cross-sections collapsing to zero and hence zero infimum of neck-pinch times, serving as an obstruction to globally flowing such data.
The end has S^2 cross-sections collapsing to zero radius so the pinch time tends to zero, obstructing a Ricci flow from all such weakly noncollapsed data.
The end has S^2 cross-sections collapsing to zero radius so singular times accumulate at 0, obstructing ordinary Ricci-flow existence.
The end with S^2 cross-sections shrinking to zero is a collapsed limiting case with zero positive existence time, used as an obstruction to globally flowing such data.
The shrinking S^2-end is collapsed at infinity with neck radii tending to zero, giving an instantaneous-pinch boundary case used as an obstruction to naively flowing every Ricci limit space.
the end has S^2 cross-sections collapsing to zero size, giving zero positive existence time and obstructing the naive strategy of flowing every Ricci limit space.
It is presented as an obstruction to global short-time Ricci flow, with S^2 cross-sections collapsing to zero size at infinity.
It obstructs flowing every weakly noncollapsed Ricci-limit/smooth space, since the S^2 fibers collapse at infinity and the corresponding pinch times tend to the zero-time boundary.
It obstructs a global-flow strategy by having an end whose spherical cross-sections collapse to zero size, forcing arbitrarily fast pinching at infinity.
Used as an obstruction to globally flowing every Ricci limit space, since the collapsed shrinking end has zero infimum pinch time at infinity.
Obstruction to global Ricci-flow existence, with a collapsed end of vanishing S^2 neck size.

Used as an obstruction to globally flowing arbitrary Ricci limit spaces; the end collapses with S^2 radius tending to zero so the singular time tends to zero at infinity, a limiting/extreme degeneration.
